title: Q&A: Where do the UN secretary general candidates stand on climate change? - Carbon Brief
gist: With Antonio Guterres stepping down end-2026, six candidates (five from Latin America, per the regional rotation) are vying to become UN secretary general; their climate stances range from Bachelet's human-rights framing and Grynspan's climate-debt focus to IAEA chief Grossi's nuclear-in-the-energy-transition pitch, with the winner decided via Security Council straw polls between August and October.

insights.claim: Antonio Guterres steps down at the end of 2026 after nearly a decade, with the next UN secretary general due to take office on 1 January 2027 and the Security Council process likely finalised between August and October 2026.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A leadership transition at the UN sets the tone for global climate diplomacy and COP dynamics into 2027, a key calendar event for climate-policy watchers.

insights.claim: Tradition dictates the role rotates by region with Latin America and the Caribbean next in line, and five of six candidates are from South or Central America, with the outlier being Senegal's Macky Sall nominated by Burundi.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Regional rotation heavily favours a Latin American winner, narrowing the field of likely climate stances and priorities.

insights.claim: An unwritten rule bars the secretary general from coming from any of the five permanent Security Council members, and selection requires clearing repeated anonymous straw polls, which took six rounds when Guterres was chosen in 2016.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The veto-gated straw-poll process means great-power preferences, not public campaigning, ultimately decide the outcome.

insights.claim: For the first time, candidates were asked to voluntarily disclose their funding sources at nomination, and a woman has never held the post in the UN's 80-year history though four of the six candidates are women.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Transparency and gender pressure are reshaping the contest, factors that could influence which candidate secures broad legitimacy.

themes.theme: Climate-finance and debt as central axis

insights.claim: All three candidates at the June debate (Bachelet, Grynspan, Espinosa) emphasised the need for finance, with Bachelet arguing debt-burdened states are drowning and should not be asked to take climate-finance loans that add to their debt.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A candidate consensus on grant-based over loan-based climate finance signals pressure to reshape multilateral climate funding toward debt relief.
insights.quote: They’re drowning and we’re asking them to ask for loans with a debt service that’s so big.

insights.claim: Grynspan, former UNCTAD chief, warns the world is sleepwalking into dangerous climate scenarios but says critical minerals are becoming as valuable as oil and supports the Bridgetown Initiative to reform global financial architecture.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A Grynspan win would foreground critical-minerals value and debt-climate financial reform, relevant to mining and development-finance investors.
insights.quote: financing is really key for any objective that we set to stick to the 1.5C target

insights.claim: Grynspan was credited with a central role in the 2022 Black Sea grain initiative that resumed blocked Russian and Ukrainian grain and fertiliser exports, with global food prices falling 23% in response.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Demonstrated deal-making on a food-security crisis is a credential that could make Grynspan a consensus pick and shape UN crisis mediation.

insights.claim: During Bachelet's second term as Chile's president, the renewables share of the energy mix grew from 6% to 17% in four years, and she enacted South America's first carbon tax plus a 70%-by-2050 renewable-energy goal.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A concrete decarbonisation record makes Bachelet the most climate-forward candidate, signalling aggressive UN climate advocacy if she wins.

insights.claim: IAEA chief Rafael Grossi claims credit for building global consensus on nuclear power in the energy transition and backed the COP28 declaration to triple nuclear energy endorsed by 22 governments including the US, France, UAE and UK.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A Grossi secretary-generalship would elevate nuclear power in climate policy, a tailwind for the nuclear and SMR sectors.

insights.claim: Grossi's vision statement does not directly mention climate or climate change and he has described the annual COP summits as unmanageable due to their size.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Grossi's downplaying of climate framing and criticism of COP scale signals a possible shift away from the Guterres-era climate-emergency emphasis.

insights.claim: During Macky Sall's presidency Senegal's oil exports surged from $0.4bn in 2015 to $2.4bn in 2024, and he was supportive of the African oil-and-gas sector with the country's first oil production site developed during his term.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Sall's pro-fossil development record contrasts sharply with the Latin American field, marking him as the least climate-restrictive candidate.

insights.claim: Espinosa, a veteran of climate COPs from Copenhagen 2009 to Paris 2015, proposes a global energy security coordination mechanism between UN members to manage energy shocks and argues the UNFCCC's consensus-based decision-making paradoxically hampers climate response.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Espinosa's critique of consensus decision-making and push for an energy-security mechanism could reshape how the UN handles energy transition governance.

insights.claim: Chile's new right-wing government under Jose Antonio Kast withdrew backing for Bachelet in March 2026 calling her nomination unviable, the first time a nominating state has withdrawn support, though Brazil and Mexico still back her.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Loss of home-state backing plus US Republican opposition materially weakens Bachelet's odds despite her strong climate record.
insights.locator: However, in March, Chile’s newly sworn-in

insights.claim: Iran opposed Grossi's candidacy for failing to condemn US-Israeli attacks on safeguarded nuclear facilities and accused him of political bias, while Sall's candidacy was rejected by AU states like Rwanda over a silent-procedure breach.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Active opposition to two candidates narrows the credible field and raises the odds of a compromise Latin American pick.
